ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tom Yum seafood rice noodle. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent. No specific Tom Yum seafood noodle code exists in FoodEx2.

main_ingredients: mixed seafood (prawns, squid, mussels or fish)

serving.description: 1 set (bowl of noodle soup with sides)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of rice noodles in tom yum seafood broth with mixed seafood, typically served as a complete meal set.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for rice noodles (cooked), mixed seafood, and tom yum broth components; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Thai noodle soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Red Tom Yum Seafood Rice Noodle Set as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked rice noodles (FDC ID 168930), mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish), and tom yum broth, weighted by approximate composition: ~45% broth, ~30% rice noodles, ~25% mixed seafood. Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai noodle soup analogues. Per-100g values reflect the blended dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles (cooked) as analogous food; GI 53 (low) for cooked rice vermicelli/rice noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Red Tom Yum Seafood Rice Noodle Set as a composite dish. GI of 53 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source — cooked rice noodles/rice vermicelli — as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The broth and seafood components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 50.6) / 100 ≈ 27.0, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving ≈ 50.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and tom yum broth
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (≈2310 mg per 550 g serving), this dish provides over 100% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce and consume the broth sparingly or avoid it entirely.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles with low GI offset by protein and broth volume
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 53 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 27 per serving, this dish poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the noodle component and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from mixed seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~2310 mg per serving) and moderate cholesterol (~55 mg per 100 g) from mixed seafood are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request low-sodium preparation, and favour lean seafood components such as fish over higher-cholesterol shellfish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Tom Yum Seafood Rice Noodle Set is a Thai noodle soup dish featuring thin rice noodles (rice vermicelli or flat rice noodles) served in a spicy, sour, and aromatic tom yum broth enriched with mixed seafood such as prawns, squid, mussels, and fish. The broth is prepared by simmering lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, bird's eye chillies, tomatoes, and mushrooms in a stock base, then seasoned with fish sauce and lime juice to achieve the characteristic hot-and-sour flavour profile of tom yum. The 'red' designation indicates the use of chilli paste or roasted chilli in oil (nam prik pao), which gives the broth a reddish colour and a deeper, slightly smoky heat compared to the clear tom yum variant. The dish is served as a set meal, typically accompanied by a side of bean sprouts, fresh herbs, and condiments. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~72 kcal per 100 g), relatively high in protein from the seafood component (~5.8 g per 100 g), low in fat (~1.4 g per 100 g), and moderate in carbohydrate from the rice noodles (~9.2 g per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content derived from fish sauce and the broth base, which can exceed 2000 mg per full serving. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on the rice noodle component. This dish is popular in Thai restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia and is suitable for halal and dairy-free diets.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Red Tom Yum Seafood Rice Noodle Set as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked rice noodles, mixed seafood, and tom yum broth, weighted by approximate composition, and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai noodle soup analogues. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no single approved-source record for this exact composite dish was identified. Values should be verified against a laboratory analysis or a future HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry if one becomes available. 2. Sodium content is particularly variable across restaurant preparations due to differing fish sauce quantities; the value of 420 mg/100 g represents a moderate restaurant estimate. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimate. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimate. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as this dish is prepared from fresh seafood and plant-based broth ingredients with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per USDA component data. 7. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 8. The input food_name contained '红冬阴海' which appears to be a partial or abbreviated Chinese rendering; the canonical Chinese name has been expanded to '红汤冬阴海鲜米粉套餐' for accuracy.
